data.title: IREN Investors: Look Beyond That $3.4B Target
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last week, upcoming neocloud provider IREN Ltd. (NASDAQ:IREN) reiterated its year-end target in its Q2 FY26 ER to achieve $3.4B in ARR as it continues to build out GPU capacity that enables it to achieve those ARR targets.

In the current investing climate prevalent for growth stocks, a mere reiteration of a prior target will result in shares getting chastised. This is what happened to IREN’s shares post-Q2 ER, leading to the neocloud company’s shares dropping 22% last week.

However, if investors cut through the post-ER pessimism, they will find enough bullish catalysts that are firmly positioning IREN to accelerate its AI services revenues further. Per my analysis, IREN is making significant advancements in three key areas—power, GPU deployments, and financing—to accelerate the neocloud business momentum it has become a beneficiary of.

The current drawdown makes IREN meaningfully attractive again, and I reiterate my bullishness on the neocloud’s prospects for this year.

Buy Any Pullback - IREN’s Buildout Will Accelerate Growth

In September last year, I wrote, “IREN timed its AI pivot to perfection,” a thesis that is playing out in its full entirety as the price of Bitcoin (BTC-USD) collapses, impacting the economics of mining Bitcoin.

For IREN, the timing of that AI pivot was extremely evident in their Q2 FY26 results, which showed just how the two business lines were diverging, not just in growth but also in predictability of forward revenue growth.

In Q2, IREN reported total revenues worth $184.7M, up 60%. That 60% pace was only possible because AI cloud services revenues surged 137% sequentially, or a mammoth 541% y/y, to $17.3M, offsetting the Bitcoin mining revenue that moved in the other direction, up 48% y/y but down 28.2% sequentially to $167.4M.

The thorn in IREN’s Q2 ER was the lack of upgrades to its revenue targets. On the call to discuss Q2 earnings, IREN’s management said:

We expect to deliver 140,000 GPUs by the end of 2026, positioning us to deliver $3.4 billion in annualized run rate revenue.

This directly signals a reiteration of the $3.4B ARR target that was announced on the Q1 FY26 call, which is something markets did not like. Also, IREN signaled that they are on track to deploy 140k GPUs by the end of this year, a GPU deployment target that was already revealed by IREN on the Q1 call. This is one of the key reasons why IREN’s shares sold off post the Q2 ER due to the lack of meaningful growth catalysts that markets were looking for.

If markets look past these two numbers, they find a goldmine of forward-looking projections that can easily build the bullish case for a strong 2026. Management did divulge plans in 3 key areas of their operating framework that robustly position IREN to be a strong neocloud beneficiary of the AI infrastructure revolution.

First, is the contracted power secured? IREN increased the volume of contracted power it secured during the quarter by adding an additional 1.6 GW of contracted power by securing a site in Oklahoma. This reflects in the amount of growth runway IREN is building for itself. To explain this from the point of view with a deeper impact, management explained:

Our $3.4 billion ARR target for the end of calendar 2026 reflects utilization of only around 10% of our 4.5 gigawatts of secured grid-connected power capacity. That means the vast majority of our portfolio remains available to support additional deployments. With demand continuing to build, that secured capacity gives us the ability to keep engaging customers on new large-scale opportunities and to extend growth well beyond the 2026 target.

Securing that much contracted power ahead of time helps IREN deploy resources to build out capacity on that contracted power grid as and when IREN signs new deals and wins new contracts. IREN also revealed plans for how quickly they are bringing online GPU capacity from this secured power grid, bringing me to point #2.

Second—volume of capacity brought online/energized. While IREN brought online ~810 MW of GPU capacity in Q2, they are on track to energize 1.4 GW of capacity in the next 3-4 months. The bulk of that forward capacity that is expected to be energized will be at IREN’s Sweetwater 1 location in Texas. That’s very positive in my view and signals that they might already be engaging with either a new hyperscaler customer or with Microsoft (MSFT) to expand their previous $9.7B five-year deal.

And third—financing and capital arrangements. Management revealed that it had already secured $3.6 billion in GPU financing at less than 6% interest, an attractive rate in today’s elevated interest rate environment. Combined with $1.9 billion in customer prepayments, this covers 95% of CapEx for their $9.7 billion Microsoft contract.

Taken together, what these three points tell me is that IREN’s management is successfully using its forward commitments from the Microsoft deal to secure financing at attractive rates to enable the company to not only secure large volumes of power capacity but also deploy resources swiftly to energize much of this power capacity and bring GPUs online faster. The end result that I see is two-fold. One, IREN wins the ability to recognize revenue from its Microsoft contract faster than expected. Two, IREN also dangles the promise of secured power and energized capacity to lure more customers who are hungry to secure more GPU capacity for their own model training/inferencing needs.

This is something markets, at the current point in time, are not recognizing, which is creating attractive entry points for IREN again, like I called out in September last year.

IREN’s Valuation Could Compress Further - But Upside Is Huge

There are two main drivers of what will move the needle for IREN’s multiple.

The first is a new ARR that IREN scores by winning new contracts, either from its current customers or from winning new customers entirely. That would push up its ARR target of $3.4B, which was probably what markets were expecting in the first place last week.

The second is if IREN surprises markets with strong revenue beats, which can only come as a result of faster-than-expected deployments of GPU capacity resulting in a quicker-than-expected pace of recognizing revenue.

Either way, IREN has enough room to win newer customer contracts, and I fully expect IREN to deliver newer wins, which could push the needle on both drivers above. That means IREN has a clear path towards being valued at ~20x forward revenues, given that its revenues are expected to accelerate every single quarter this year, with the December quarter expected to demonstrate a 290% jump in revenues to $721M.

In the short term, pessimism might persist, putting pressure on IREN’s forward revenue multiple, pushing it down to ~10x revenues. But if investors maintain a year-long horizon, any pullbacks will be strong opportunities to load up on IREN.

To summarize, short-term pessimism may push IREN’s forward revenue multiple to 10x, implying ~20% downside, but I expect IREN’s strong growth plans to eventually be rewarded by markets, leading to expansion in the forward revenue multiple to 20x, implying ~53% upside.

Risks & Other Factors To Know

As mentioned above, over the next few months, IREN’s forward revenue multiple may face pressure due to the lack of updates to its $3.4B ARR target. But I do expect the outlook to improve as management’s action plan signals strong runways of growth this year. That’s based on the backdrop that hyperscalers continue to portion their capex towards neoclouds like IREN, who carry the execution risk of building out GPU capacity for hyperscalers.

The outlook for neoclouds like IREN is strong, with industry research projecting neoclouds like IREN to grow at a 69% CAGR. IREN is growing much faster than that. In the event that demand falls or IREN fails to execute on its action plans, IREN’s shares will be significantly impacted.

Takeaway

I believe IREN’s shares continue to look interesting despite the short hiccup that its Q2 FY26 ER presented last week. At 13x forward revenues, IREN’s shares look very appealing in terms of the risk/reward, leading me to reiterate my bullish views on IREN’s shares.

data.title: Market Sector Review: Extreme Market Bifurcation
data.description:
Since the beginning of the year, we have discussed the “reflation trade” and its impact on specific market sectors. This past weekend’s newsletter also showed some of these more extreme returns in various market sectors since the beginning of the year. To wit:

“Despite what seemed like a rough week in the market, it really wasn’t as most sectors and markets, outside of technology, moved into very overbought territory on a short-term basis. Energy, Materials, Industrials, and Staples, or rather “value,” have strongly outperformed every other market and sector this year. These areas are the most overbought, and a rotation back to growth seems increasingly obvious.”

There are several key takeaways from the analysis below.

  1. Since the beginning of 2026, Staples (up 15%), Industrials (up 12%), Energy (up 21%), and Materials (up 17%) have vastly outperformed the market as a whole, which is effectively flat YTD.
  2. The performance differential of those sectors versus the markets, and the deviations from their 50-day moving averages, are at extremes.
  3. While the overall market has been trading weakly since January, that is only a function of the largest market sectors, by capitalization, underperforming and are now oversold.

As shown below, those market sectors make up a relatively small portion of the overall index: Basic Materials (1.93%), Industrials (8.26%), Energy (3.11%), and Staples (5.76%). In other words, those 4 sectors combined (~19%) are smaller than the Technology sector alone (~29%). This also suggests that the relative outperformance of those sectors in recent weeks has more than offset the weakness in the Technology sector.

The breadth of those market sectors has also been extremely strong, with very high percentages of stocks in those sectors trading above their respective 50-, 100-, and 200-day moving averages, versus very low percentages in Communications, Technology, and Financials.

While the current outperformance of those select market sectors has been very enticing this year, the risk to investors comes if the “Reflation Narrative” falters for any reason. Those risks could come from a stronger dollar, falling inflation, or the realization that the overvaluation of these sectors has reached levels more historically extreme, given that these sectors have historically had some of the weakest revenue growth, particularly Energy.

With that said, since today is “Presidents’ Day” and the markets are closed, we will go through a technical review of each S&P 500 market sector, with a brief trading setup heading into next week.

How To Read The Charts

Using the S&P 500 Index as an example, this brief tutorial explains how to read the charts.

  1. The red line is the daily price for the past 3 years.
  2. The shaded areas are Bollinger Bands, which track the price’s standard deviation relative to the underlying moving average. In our examples, we are measuring price at 2 and 3 standard deviations from the 50-day moving average.
  3. The green line is the 200-day moving average.
  4. The shaded grey area in the background is a Williams %R reading, which measures short-term overbought and oversold conditions.
  5. The bottom graph is a Relative Strength Index, which also measures overbought and oversold conditions.

Okay, let’s get to it.

Sector Review

Basic Materials

  • Basic Materials broke through previous resistance and is now 3 standard deviations above the moving average. However, the trend remains bullish.
  • Currently, the price is very deviated above the rising 200-day, and extension risk is high after a steep run into 53 to 54.
  • With both longer-term readings at more extreme overbought levels, the risk of a correction is rising.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at the 50-dma, which is now critical support.
  • Action plan: Trim into strength, raise stops under 52 for tactical positions. Longer-term trend stop is near 47 to 48. Avoid new buys until 52 or 50 holds.

Staples

  • Like XLB, Staples has also surged through previous resistance and is now well into 3 standard deviations above the moving average.
  • Currently, the price is very deviated above the rising 200-day, and extension risk is high after a steep run into 88 to 90.
  • With both longer-term readings at more extreme overbought levels, the risk of a correction is rising.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at the 50-dma, which is now critical support and sits near the previous breakout levels.
  • Action plan: Trim into strength, raise stops under 82 for tactical positions. Longer-term trend stop is near 79 to 80. Avoid new buys until a correction holds at either previous breakout levels or the 200-day moving average.

Industrials

  • The chase also continues in Industrials, which is also 3 standard deviations above its mean. If you review the chart carefully, you will see that while these deviations can persist for some time, they eventually revert.
  • Currently, the price is very deviated above the rising 200-day, and extension risk is high after a steep run into 173 to 174.
  • With both longer-term readings at more extreme overbought levels, the risk of a correction is rising.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at the 50-dma, but critical support remains at the previous breakout levels at 155.
  • Action plan: Trim into strength, raise stops under 163 for tactical positions. Longer-term trend stop is near 153. Avoid new buys until a correction holds at either previous breakout levels or the 200-day moving average.

Financials

  • Unlike our previous market sectors, the Financials sector has been under considerable pressure. XLF is now 3 standard deviations below its mean, and is at levels that have previously constituted buying opportunities.
  • However, the break below the 200-day moving average does provide near-term risk. Hold off on purchases until the moving average is recovered.
  • With both longer-term readings nearing oversold levels, the risk of a deeper correction is subsiding.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 50, which is critical support from previous lows.
  • Action plan: Keep a watch on financials for a recovery that reclaims and holds the 200-day moving average. Keep stops very close to current levels.

Consumer Discretionary

  • Like XLF, the Discretionary sector has also been under considerable pressure. XLY has broken through its 50-day moving average and is approaching critical support at its 200-day moving average. Unlike XLF, XLY is not nearly as oversold, so the sector currently has more downside risk. Remain cautious with positioning.
  • As noted, the 200-day moving average provides near-term support, and XLY needs to hold those levels to maintain its bullish trend.
  • Both longer-term readings are approaching oversold levels, but not there yet, which suggests downside risks persist.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 115, which is the 200-day moving average.
  • Action plan: Keep a watch on the discretionary to see if it can hold the 200-day moving average. Keep stops at that level for now until price action improves.

Healthcare

  • The Healthcare sector remains in more neutral territory. It is neither grossly extended nor oversold, which keeps positioning stable for now.
  • However, the deviation above the 200-day moving average is extreme, and the sector could be forming a top at current levels.
  • With RSI at a more neutral level, keep holdings at current target weights but be mindful of risks.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 150, which is the previous sell-off low.
  • Action plan: Hold healthcare positions for now, but maintain around 150. If a rotation out of healthcare begins, it is a decent drop to the 200-day moving average.

Transportation

  • Transportation stocks have been on a massive run since the “Liberation Day” lows last year. Transports recently peaked 3 standard deviations above its mean and appears to have started a short-term correction.
  • However, the deviation above the 200-day moving average remains significant, so tighten up stops and take profits in the sector if you have not already.
  • Both longer-term readings are starting to reverse from overbought levels, which potentially adds to current risk levels.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 95, the previous support level.
  • Action plan: Take profits and rebalance the sector back to target weights. Keep stops on trading positions at the 50-day moving average with an understanding there is risk down to the 200-day.

Technology

  • Technology has been the poster child of the selloff recently. Notably, the technology sector is trying to hold support at lows from last November. A failure to do so would imply potentially more downside risk.
  • Notably, the 200-day moving average is a strong support level for both traders and investors.
  • With both longer-term readings nearing oversold levels, the risk of a deeper correction is subsiding.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 135, which is the 200-day moving average.
  • Action plan: If you failed to take profits previously in technology, it is a little late to do that now. Hold current positions with a stop at the 200-day moving average. Look to add to holdings if the sector can begin to improve relative strength, reclaim the 50-day moving average, and start to show an improvement in money flows.

Utilities

  • As we discussed last year, the Utilities sector was hated, and no one wanted to own the “boring” stuff. That tone has changed this year, with XLU surging through resistance and pushing well above the 3-standard-deviation level.
  • However, the recent breakout to new highs for XLU is encouraging and could foster further buying. However, the extension does pose a trading risk.
  • With both longer-term readings overbought, higher prices are likely to become more problematic.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at the 200-day moving average, which is key support.
  • Action plan: Utilities are caught up in the reflation narrative, but the fundamentals don’t support the speculation. Hold positions but keep stops at the 200-day moving average.

Real Estate

  • Like XLU, Real Estate has also seen a sudden surge in buying interest, and money rotates from growth to value. However, a lot of the stocks in these sectors are trading more like growth than value. The extreme deviation above the mean in XLRE, like XLU, is a warning, but it can persist for some time. So hold positions but manage your risk.
  • However, the 200-day moving average is nearby and provides a useful benchmark for both traders and investors to manage their risk exposures.
  • With both longer-term readings nearing overbought levels, the risk of a correction is rising.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 41, which is the 200-day moving average for now.
  • Action plan: Keep a watch on real estate for buyer exhaustion as the run is getting extended. Hold positions for now, but taking profits and rebalancing to target weights never hurts. The 200-day moving average remains key support.

Conclusion

The current bifurcation inside the S&P 500 raises investor risk as price leadership has shifted toward sectors with weaker fundamental momentum, as noted above. Energy, Utilities, Industrials, and Materials work best when inflation rises, rates rise, and growth stays steady enough to support pricing power. That is the “reflation trade” narrative, but recent economic reports are undermining it as growth slows, inflation and wages fall, and rates decline.

Furthermore, given that markets eventually trade on revenue and earnings growth rates, which foster valuation premiums, the Technology, Communications, and parts of Discretionary are seeing significant discounts to expected growth rates. As such, when capital crowds into slow-growth defensives and cyclical inflation hedges while selling the higher-growth complex, the index loses internal quality. As we have seen, breadth narrows, volatility rises, broad market rallies shorten, and drawdowns get faster once the rotation exhausts.

This setup also increases policy risk. If inflation data continues to cool or the market reprices the path of rates lower, the reflation trade tends to unwind quickly. Energy and Materials face demand risk; Industrials face order risk; and Utilities are exposed to rate sensitivity, especially if yields fall quickly after a risk event. At the same time, the growth sectors being sold often have the strongest balance sheets and the highest margins, so when selling pressure hits those areas, it often reflects de-risking rather than a negative impact on future growth.

If you are overexposed to the reflation narrative, consider taking some gains and reducing concentration. Tightening risk controls, rebalancing toward target weights, and starting to focus on quality growth and cash flow durability is a good start. If you are overexposed to growth, focus on trimming the weakest balance sheets first, then add back only after price stabilizes and leadership improves. The goal is balance, not a single macro bet.

Trade accordingly.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nebius Group Q4 Earnings: My CliffsNotes On Revenue Recognition
data.description:
Nebius Group Trades at 800+ P/E With FQ4 Earnings

Nebius Group, Inc. (NBIS) recently released their earnings report (ER) on Feb 12, 2026. As a battlefront AI infrastructure stock, many aspects of the ER have been quickly dissected by many other analysts. As such, in this article, I will focus on 3 aspects that are less discussed yet important to the understanding of high-growth stocks: negative accounting EPS, revenue recognition, and surging CAPEX. My goals are to explain A) why the extreme headline P/E ratios can be very misleading because of the difference between accounting EPS and cash flow and B) why the record CAPEX is more concerning in comparison with the EPS and/or P/E ratios, especially when combined with the company’s balance sheet changes.

Let me start with the issues of its EPS and P/E ratios. With the FQ4 ER, NBIS reported a quarterly loss of $0.99 in terms of GAAP EPS, leading to a TTM P/E ratio of 855x on a GAAP basis, as seen in the next chart below. The EPS is negative on non-GAAP, thus resulting in no meaningful P/E ratios. Next, I will examine these accounting EPS figures more closely and explain why they paint a very poor picture of Nebius’ true earnings.

NBIS: Disconnect Between Accounting EPS and True Cash Flow

In my view, the reconciliation section of the cash flow statement is where the magic or the mess happens. As the section bridges the gap between the Accounting Net Income (which follows accrual rules) and the actual cash that hit the company's bank account. The essence of stock analysis is large to understand this gap in my experiences. If you share similar experiences, you can see why NBIS’ Q4 reconciliation caught my attention. Two items - Accounts Receivable (A/R) and Unearned Revenue (UR)—are particularly noteworthy, and they are shown in the screenshot below (note that I skipped several line items in the screenshot to minimize clustering). These items created the biggest divergence between the EPS you see in the headlines and the company's true profits in Q4, in my assessment.

Starting with A/R, it represents money that customers owe Nebius for services already delivered (like GPU compute time) but haven't paid for yet. This boosts Net Income and EPS, even if they haven't received a single cent in cash. As such, on the cash flow statement, an increase in A/R is subtracted from the Net Income as seen. And this is the second-largest reconciliation item in Q4, as seen at -$632 million.

Now moving on to the UR, this is the opposite of A/R in the sense that it’s prepayment cash Nebius has received from customers for services they haven't provided yet (e.g., a customer paying upfront for a year of reserved H100 capacity). Because the service hasn't been delivered, GAAP accounting rules do not allow this to be counted as earnings and therefore do not help the EPS. But it is cash received, and the cash flow statement recognizes this by an increase in UR and adds it back to Net Income. The change in UR is the largest reconciliation item in Q4, as seen at $1,566 million.

With these reconciliations, the company’s operating cash flow is actually not only positive but also quite sizable for the quarter at $834 million.

Nebius Stock: CAPEX Surge

Understanding the above reconciliation practice also helps me to better contextualize NBIS’ CAPEX expansion plan. As background, the company spent about $955 million in Q3 on capital projects, as seen in the next chart below. The figure surged to more than $2 billion in Q4. Given its expansion plan announced in the ER (see more details quoted below), I expect elevated CAPEX to persist in the near future given its goal to reach 1 GW of connected power and a large data center in France.

Seeking Alpha News: CEO Volozh noted that in the fourth quarter of 2025, capital expenditures were about $2.1B, mainly driven by purchases of GPUs and GPU-related hardware and its data center expansion activities, including investments in planned greenfield sites. The company noted that it remains on track to reach 800 MW to 1 GW of connected power by the end of 2026. The company now expects to have more than 3 GW of contracted power by year-end. Nebius is planning a new 240 MW data center in Béthune, France, that will be one of Europe’s largest when it is finished.

The Q4 CAPEX surge could seem extreme. For example, it exceeds the total revenue the company reported in the quarter (of around $227 million) by almost 10-fold. However, with an understanding of the differences between accounting earnings and true cash flow, the picture is far less extreme—but still quite stretched, though. Q4’s CAPEX of around $2B is about 2.4x the operating cash flow of $384 million.

Going forward, the picture could further improve as the company’s Annual Recurring Revenue (or ARR) grows. For example, with the Q4 ER, the company’s ARR as of year-end was $1.25B, ahead of its most recent guidance in the range of $900M to $1.1B. As the company keeps growing its AI infrastructure business, the impacts of revenue recognition could become even larger and more crucial for investors to understand its true economic earnings and CAPEX sustainability.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

As just mentioned, the CAPEX surge is far less extreme than what appears on the surface, but it is still quite stretched in my view and exceeds the organic cash flow by a wide margin. As a result, the company has been relying on debt financing, whose role has been increasing at a rapid pace and warrants close monitoring. Per its Q4 balance sheet as shown below, its debt has expanded dramatically to support its pivot into AI infrastructure. At the end of Q4, total liabilities reached approximately $7.8 billion. In contrast, the reading was almost negligible ($295 million) as reported a year ago and was “only” $5.3 billion even a quarter ago. This growth is primarily driven by the surge in long-term debt, which stands at $4.1 billion now. The company had no long-term debt till the June quarter of 2025. These obligations, combined with the cash flow deficit just mentioned, could impact its CAPEX sustainability as the company scales.

To close, this NBIS analysis focused on its fourth quarter 2025 ER with an emphasis on 3 items less discussed: negative GAAP EPS, revenue recognition, and surging CAPEX. An examination of these items has led me to see a wide gap between its GAAP EPS and true cash flows and believe the CAPEX surge is less concerning than on the surface. As the company keeps growing its ARR at rapid rates, I recommend investors pay close attention to how the company adjusts its future revenues (and manages its balance sheet too).

data.title: Midstream MLPs: High Income With Equity Characteristics
data.description:
Master Limited Partnerships in the energy midstream sector have long appealed to income investors because of their ability to generate steady, fee-based cash flows from transporting oil and gas. Unlike upstream producers, pipeline operators are largely insulated from commodity price volatility, relying instead on contracted volumes and long-lived infrastructure assets. That stability has historically supported elevated distributions relative to many other income vehicles.¹

In recent years, the financial profile of the midstream sector has improved meaningfully. Balance sheets have strengthened, leverage has declined, and distribution coverage has become more conservative. These changes have helped make payouts more durable than they were during prior energy cycles. Still, MLPs remain equity securities. Their distributions are not contractual obligations and can be adjusted if business conditions deteriorate. Unit prices also fluctuate with broader equity markets and shifts in energy sentiment.

The distinguishing advantage of MLPs is flexibility. As infrastructure utilization rises and cash flows expand, distributions can grow over time. That growth potential differentiates midstream assets from fixed-income securities, whose payments are static by design.² For investors willing to tolerate equity risk, MLPs offer a combination of income and long-term cash-flow participation that traditional bonds cannot replicate.

Preferred Stocks and Bonds: Stability With Trade-Offs

Preferred stocks occupy a middle ground between bonds and common equity. They typically pay fixed dividends and rank above common shares in the capital structure but below bonds. Recent interest rate volatility pushed preferred prices lower, lifting yields across much of the market. That higher income comes with limitations. Preferred dividends can be suspended without triggering default, and holders have limited upside and no voting rights.³

REIT preferreds add another layer of complexity. Many issues are cumulative, meaning missed dividends must be paid before common distributions resume. However, because REITs do not pay corporate income taxes, their preferred dividends are generally taxed as ordinary income rather than qualifying for reduced dividend rates. This distinction can materially affect after-tax income depending on an investor’s tax situation.⁴

Corporate bonds remain the most traditional income option. Investment-grade bonds provide predictable cash flows and principal repayment at maturity, offering stability that appeals to conservative investors. Their yields tend to be lower than those of MLPs or preferreds, reflecting their higher claim on assets and contractual payment structure. High-yield bonds narrow the income gap but introduce credit risk and limited upside.⁵

Yield, Risk, and Taxes: Choosing the Right Mix

Taxes are a critical differentiator across income strategies. A substantial portion of MLP distributions is typically classified as return of capital, allowing taxes to be deferred until units are sold. This feature can significantly enhance after-tax income for long-term holders, though it adds complexity through K-1 filings and potential multi-state tax exposure.⁶ Preferred dividends from taxable corporations may qualify for favorable tax treatment, while bond interest is fully taxable unless held in tax-advantaged accounts.

Ultimately, the choice between midstream MLPs, preferred stocks, and corporate bonds depends on an investor’s objectives and risk tolerance. Bonds offer predictability and capital stability. Preferreds provide incremental yield with moderate structural risk. MLPs deliver higher income potential and tax efficiency, balanced by equity market exposure and sector-specific risks.

Rather than viewing these options as substitutes, many income-focused portfolios blend them. Diversifying across contractual income, hybrid securities, and equity-based cash flows can help smooth results across market environments while preserving flexibility as conditions evolve.

Footnotes

  1. Meketa Investment Group, Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs) (June 2022).

  2. Hennessy Funds, “What’s Driving Midstream Company Performance?” (January 2025).

  3. Charles Schwab, “Are Preferred Securities Still Attractive?” (January 28, 2025).

  4. Innovative Income Investor, “Preferred Stocks of REITs” (December 28, 2025).

  5. Charles Schwab, “Corporate Bonds and Portfolio Stability” (2025).

  6. ETF Database (VettaFi), “MLP Distributions Versus Corporate Dividends” (August 11, 2022).

data.title: OFS Credit: Stick To The Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

OFS Credit (OCCI) is an externally managed closed-end fund, focusing on CLO equity and debt. While the CEF does contain some CLO debt, the very vast majority of the assets consists of CLO equity, as OFS Credit focuses on the higher-yielding securities. The weighted average effective yield of the investments was approximately 13%.

A look at the full-year results

During its financial year, OFS Credit had to navigate a tougher credit environment as Morningstar reported the total default rates doubled from 0.73% to 1.46%, backing off from a multi-year high at 1.50% at the end of the third quarter in the calendar year 2025. Meanwhile, the CLO market is still booming as the total amount of private credit offerings increased by a high single digit percentage during the year.

Looking at the FY 2025 results, we see OFS reported an interest income of $44.2M, while the total amount of operating expenses came in at almost $22M. Approximately 1/3 rd of the total operating expenses are related to the interest expenses, with the combination of incentive fees and base management fees making up about half of the expenses.

This resulted in a net investment income of $22.6M, and divided over the weighted average share count throughout the year, this represented a NII/share of around 88 cents. A good result, but the statement of operations also shows there was a $3.6M realized loss, while the total fair value of its portfolio decreased by almost $29M.

As OFS Credit is a ‘serial ATM issuer,’ its share count continued to increase throughout the year. At the end of October, OFS had just over 28M shares outstanding, resulting in a NAV/share of $5.46.

That's, of course, almost four months ago, and in a more recent update, OFS Credit has disclosed the current NAV/share is approximately $4.31-4.41.

At the end of October, the balance sheet contained $271M in assets, backed by $114M in preferred stock. As these are term preferred shares with mandatory redemption dates, the preferred shares are listed as a liability rather than an equity component.

What does this mean for the fixed income securities?

I rarely invest in the common equity of CLO equity-focused CEFs. I’d rather invest in a more senior security and accept lower distributions versus running the risks associated with being first in line in case things go south. Additionally, OFS Credit’s continuous share printing program erodes the value of the common shares.

OFS Credit has five series of preferred shares outstanding, shown below.

Three of these issues are currently listed. The 6.125% Series C term preferred is trading with OCCIO as its ticker symbol and currently trades around par. I’d say this is the lowest-risk security, as the mandatory redemption date is at the end of April. As this security pays its preferred dividend in monthly installments, you could look at OCCIO as some sort of ‘term deposit,’ collecting a 1% return in dividend payments. While there are no guarantees, I don’t expect to see any major issues that would prohibit or delay the repayment of these securities.

I’m also keeping an eye on the Series E preferred stock, trading with OCCIN as its ticker symbol. This issue has a lower preferred dividend coupon of just 5.25%, but matures at the end of this year. At the current share price of $24.56, this would result in a capital gain of 1.8% in the next ten months.

Investment thesis

While I’m personally not interested in initiating a long position in the common units issued by OFS Credit, I do acknowledge certain investors could be interested in the high yield, but the NAV erosion just goes too fast for my liking. Additionally, as interest rates may move down a bit more in the near future, the default risk should decrease (although this will likely go hand in hand with lower returns on the investments).

I’m focusing on the senior securities issued by OFS Credit, as I want the additional layer of safety. Although OCCIO will be retired in just over two months, it’s an interesting vehicle to park some cash (subject to reasonable transaction expenses, of course).

I currently have no position in any of OFS’ preferred stock, but I certainly would consider a long position when a good opportunity arises.

data.title: Teradata: AI Tailwinds Could Create Further Upside
data.description:
Teradata's (TDC) share price has surged in recent months, driven by emerging AI tailwinds, which isn't necessarily surprising given the amount of investment going into the infrastructure layer at the moment. Recent share price strength is more a product of the demand environment than anything company-specific, though.

I previously suggested that Teradata was yet to stem on-prem losses and that its cloud growth was insufficient to drive topline growth. The stock is fairly flat since then, even after the recent surge. Demand appears to have strengthened to the point that Teradata could feasibly return to consistent growth, though.

Teradata's valuation is relatively low, meaning that there is still room for significant upside if investors begin to price in growth and improving margins. Despite this, if AI fails to boost the business, the stock could still fall significantly. I expect Teradata's business to accelerate over the next 12 months, but I am cautious about the stock because it is unclear how sustainable this situation is.

Market Conditions

Teradata has suggested that the demand environment is stable at the moment, with its hybrid cloud plus on-prem deployment model continuing to appeal to customers in regulated industries and where data sovereignty is important. The company also believes that there is an opportunity to displace legacy vendors and capture customers reconsidering cloud-only strategies.

Teradata's near-term prospects are likely to hinge on the extent to which it delivers on-prem AI solutions to its customer base. AI is viewed as a new TAM expansion, not just incremental optimization, and could help the company return to consistent growth.

Services revenue is transitioning from cloud migration projects to AI-focused engagements, which could be a harbinger of stronger growth in coming years. This is supported by robust proof-of-concept activity, some of which is moving into production. There is little evidence that this is helping Teradata to expand its customer base, though.

Teradata Business Updates

Teradata's solutions can be broadly grouped under cloud and on-prem data warehousing, business analytics, and consulting. The company's analytics and data platform enables a multi-cloud approach (on-prem, hybrid, private cloud, and multi-cloud). Teradata also offers solutions that help customers to prepare data and train and deploy models using highly optimized in-database functions.

Teradata is positioning itself as an integrated platform spanning cloud and on-prem that provides customers the ability to run agentic AI without moving data. Newer features in support of this include a vector store, enhanced ModelOps, an agent builder, and pre-built agents. Teradata is also providing forward-deployed engineering services to move POCs to production. While this could enable the company to capitalize on the AI opportunity, competition is fierce, and Teradata has had difficulties adjusting from its prior focus on on-prem deployments.

Financial Analysis

Teradata generated 421 million USD in revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 3% YoY, with growth largely driven by public cloud. Teradata has suggested that on-prem AI will be a growth driver going forward, although this appears to be largely at the proof-of-concept stage at the moment. Investors should look for services revenue to increase as these projects move beyond POC before ARR begins to expand.

Teradata is guiding to 2-4% total ARR growth in 2026. Recurring revenue growth is expected to be in the 0-2% range, while total revenue is expected to be flat to down 2%. Revenue guidance is stronger for Q1, suggesting either that tailwinds are expected to fade as the year progresses or that full-year guidance is overly conservative. Teradata's next-gen on-prem platform with embedded GPUs could also provide a tailwind later in 2026, given that minimal contribution has been baked into guidance.

Teradata's cloud ARR growth has decelerated sharply over the past few years, although the fourth quarter could represent a reversal to this trend. The company had a little over 1.5 billion USD ARR at the end of 2025, up 3% YoY, including 701 million USD Public Cloud ARR (15% growth and a 108% Cloud Net Expansion Rate).

It is worthwhile noting that Teradata's cloud growth has fallen well short of internal expectations. For example, in early 2024, Teradata was targeting 1 billion USD in cloud ARR by the end of 2025. This should be taken into consideration when assessing current management commentary around the company's AI prospects.

Teradata's non-GAAP Product gross margin is currently around 66%, while consulting gross margin has improved to 19%. This is notable as the company's consulting gross margin had been hovering near zero. On the hardware side, memory costs could become an issue at some point, although Teradata has managed to navigate this situation so far.

Teradata's adjusted EBITDA margin was 28% in Q4 and has been steadily improving. The company also generated 285 million USD in free cash flow in 2025 and plans on directing 50% of free cash flow toward buybacks going forward. Teradata is targeting 310-330 million USD in free cash flow in 2026, and its margins should continue to improve if recurring revenue growth persists.

A recent rebound in hiring means that if growth fails to improve, margins will likely come under pressure. I take the increase in job openings to mean that the company has confidence in its forward prospects, though.

Conclusion

While Teradata has had some success with its cloud business, this has been on-premise-to-cloud transitions. Stabilization of on-prem ARR and AI demand tailwinds could mean that this situation is changing, though. I expect modest growth and improving margins in the near term, which could create further upside.

There is uncertainty, though. Recent strength is due to the demand environment, and I continue to have little confidence in Teradata long-term. The company still needs to demonstrate that recent improvements are sustainable and that its newer initiatives will have a material impact.

It should also be kept in mind that rapid advances in AI are causing investors to reassess the competitive positioning of many companies, and Teradata could easily become a victim of these. In addition, the recent pullback in software stocks means that there are many companies with far better prospects that are now trading on similar revenue multiples to Teradata.

data.title: Impax U.S. Sustainable Economy Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

  • The US Sustainable Economy portfolio outperformed the Russell 1000 benchmark during the quarter. Excess returns were driven by a combination of sustainability tilts, and stock specific effects, with style factors marginally detracting. Sector and industry, which are an outcome of the portfolio's sustainability profile, were additive. Stock-specific effects were greatest in Health Care, as pharmaceutical companies including Eli Lilly (LLY) saw outsized gains. Financials were another area of positive stock-specific effects with payment processor Visa (V) and asset manager State Street performing well. Security selection was challenged in Consumer Staples with packaged food names Campbell's (CPB) and General Mills (GIS), as well as household products holding Clorox (CLX), detracting. The portfolio's style factor tilts detracted marginally as the portfolio's higher quality profile was not rewarded, as quality factors saw continued headwinds. The portfolio's lower risk profile was marginally additive as the lower exposure to stocks with higher residual volatility contributed positively, while the lower beta profile offset this somewhat. Exposure to value factors was also marginally positive as stocks with lower price-to-earnings ratios (PE) outperformed higher PE stocks in the period, while the portfolio's slight tilt away from momentum detracted.
  • The sustainability tools were a key driver of portfolio performance in the period, with industry tilts from the Sustainability Lens and our focus on companies with attractive Corporate Resilience profiles both benefiting the portfolio.

Market overview

  • US equity markets, as measured by the Russell 1000, ended the quarter modestly higher. The period was marked by bouts of volatility, driven by concerns over elevated valuations and uncertainty surrounding returns and financing for large-scale data center investments. These factors prompted profit-taking in certain mega-cap technology and AI-related companies, although the market overall remained resilient. Market leadership shifted toward Health Care, which rallied following the Trump administration's agreement with major pharmaceutical firms to reduce Medicaid drug prices. Pro-cyclical sectors also outperformed, with Materials buoyed by commodity strength and Financials expected to benefit from looser monetary conditions. Small caps also performed strongly, reaching new highs, as investors seek out attractive valuations.
  • Softer labor market data and signs of consumer weakness in residential housing led the Federal Reserve (Fed) to deliver two additional interest rate cuts during the quarter, despite persistent inflationary pressures. While some economic indicators softened, US Q3 GDP growth surprised to the upside and corporate earnings remained robust. A new Fed Chair in 2026 is likely to maintain a 'dovish' stance, with further rate cuts highly likely through the year.
  • As we enter 2026, our macroeconomic outlook is one of cautious optimism. Falling interest rates and rising government spending should provide support for risk assets. However, narrow drivers of economic growth and unsustainable fiscal trajectories remain areas of concern. Our investment approach continues to be to build a diversified portfolio of businesses with strong long-term secular growth drivers, sound corporate resilience management, and robust sustainability characteristics.

Contributors

  • Eli Lilly (Health Care, Pharmaceuticals) is included due to its strategic positioning with respect to sustainability opportunities, specifically in HealthCare Access and Innovation. Eli Lilly announced a significant agreement with the Trump administration aimed at improving patient access and reducing prices for select medications. A key development from this agreement is the extended coverage of GLP-1 weight loss drugs within Medicare and Medicaid programs. This announcement was well received by investors, as it alleviated ongoing concerns about stringent drug pricing and created a substantial new market opportunity for GLP-1s.
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) (Information Technology, Semiconductors) is owned for its exceptional Corporate Resilience profile and attractive sustainability opportunity set in areas like the Digital Infrastructure build-out. The stock surged on the back of strong demand for AI-optimized chips and data center processors, which significantly outperformed market expectations. The company also benefited from major partnerships with 'hyperscale' cloud computing providers and record sales of graphics processing units (GPUs) for AI workloads, reinforcing investor confidence in its growth trajectory.
  • Merck (MRK) (Health Care, Pharmaceuticals) demonstrates outstanding Corporate Resilience across governance, environmental and social dimensions. The organization is strategically positioned to leverage opportunities in Health Care Access and Innovation. During the quarter, Merck's shares advanced due to robust sales of its oncology and immunology medications, notably Keytruda, which continued to expand its global market share. Positive clinical trial results and an upward adjustment to 2026 earnings guidance further enhanced market confidence.

Detractors

  • Oracle (ORCL) (Information Technology, Systems Software) stands out with a strong sustainability profile, offering notable opportunities in Digital Infrastructure, Education and the development of Enhanced Skills & Innovation. Recently, the stock declined due to worries that the company is accumulating excessive debt to finance its AI data center expansion supported by OpenAI.
  • Zoetis (ZTS) (Health Care, Pharmaceuticals) is held due to its attractive sustainability opportunity profile, as well as its strong governance profile. There was some disappointment in their Q3 earnings update in November. Zoetis has a full pipeline of new products to launch, but there is some time yet until these products can hit the market and accelerate revenue growth (2027 onwards). In the meantime, they face competition from others to some of their established franchises. Key canine arthritis pain product Librela continues to face side-effect perception issues and declined by more than expected in Q3, leading to disappointment in the quarter.
  • Alphabet Class C (GOOG) (Communication Services, Interactive Media & Services), an underweight position, was one of the top performers in the period. The position is held for its exposure to Digital Infrastructure and Education, Enhanced Skills & Innovation opportunities. Share price performance was driven by robust advertising revenue growth, particularly from YouTube and Search, as digital advertising spending rebounded during the holiday season. Cloud revenues saw double-digit growth and margin improvement, while cost optimization initiatives boosted profitability, leading to positive investor sentiment. The portfolio was overweight the Class A shares at the end of the period.

GICS sector attribution#

  • Rather than following a single risk-on trend, sector performance shifted among various themes such as interest rates, AI, defensives and commodity sensitivity. Only two sectors outperformed the index: Health Care, which benefited from its defensive characteristics and attractive valuations that drew inflows during periods of market uncertainty; and Communication Services, which was supported by Google's AI breakthroughs and potential merger and acquisition (M&A) activity involving Warner Brothers Discovery. Financials delivered mixed results, with capital markets outperforming traditional lenders after the December rate cut. Information Technology (IT) experienced some AI-related indigestion in December due to ongoing concerns about elevated capital expenditures, while Industrials gains were concentrated primarily in segments like logistics, aerospace and defense. Consumer Discretionary performance was divided, with areas supporting higher income consumers showing some resilience, while credit-sensitive stocks tended to weaken in line with softer earnings outlooks. Consumer Staples underperformed as investors weighed the impact of higher prices on middle- and lower-income consumers. Materials were driven by metals and mining equities, reflecting sustained strength in industrial commodity prices. Real Estate, which is typically sensitive to interest rates, might have been expected to benefit from easing rate expectations but continued to face headwinds related to long-term financing concerns. Utilities experienced profit-taking after electric utility stocks delivered strong returns over the calendar year.
  • The portfolio's modest sector tilts aided performance this quarter. Overweighting Health Care (notably Pharmaceuticals and Biotechnology) contributed positively, as did underweight positions in Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary. Limited sustainability opportunities in Communication Services and an overweight to IT detracted from results.

Sustainability tools

Impax Sustainability Lens

The Lens is used to align the portfolio's industry exposure to the tailwinds associated with the transition to a more sustainable economy by ranking sub-industries based on the exposure to themes, like Education, Enhanced Skills & Innovation, Meeting Basic Needs, and Addressing Climate Change and Pollution. The portfolio's exposure to the Impax Sustainability Lens contributed positively during the period, as the overweight to stocks in higher opportunity sub-industries was additive. Exposure to high opportunity Health Care industries, like Pharmaceuticals and Biotechnology, was additive, as was exposure to the semiconductor value chain and application software.

Corporate Resilience Analysis

Impax assesses a company's resilience to a variety of industry and company-specific factors. The team's research has found that companies that do not manage governance, the environment, or social risks effectively exhibit more volatility than those with more resilience over the long term. The portfolio maintains an overweight position in stocks with higher Corporate Resilience scores and excludes those with lower scores. This approach contributed positively to performance in the final quarter, with companies scoring highly on Corporate Resilience outperforming the broader market, while those with the lowest scores in the Russell 1000 significantly lagged. The pronounced underweight allocations to stocks with poor Corporate Resilience scores, such as Meta and Palantir – which were excluded due to unfavorable profiles – positively contributed to relative performance, particularly as these stocks generated negative results in an otherwise moderately positive market environment. Conversely, active holdings in high-scoring companies further supported portfolio performance.

Fund overview

Impax US Sustainable Economy Fund is a systematic strategy investing in US large cap companies we believe are positioned to benefit from the transition to a more sustainable economy.

data.title: Pacer Data And Digital Revolution ETF: Another Copycat ETF, No Interest
data.description:
The ticker symbol for the Pacer Data and Digital Revolution ETF (TRFK) is, to me, more about "traffic" than "terrific." I say that tongue-in-cheek, but there's a serious point to be made. And it's not just about this ETF. It's about the industry, broadly.

I'm as big a fan of the ETF structure as you will find. But Wall Street has again done what it tends to do with any good idea: it bludgeons it by overdoing it. Too many products is not a problem. Too many products that do not differentiate from each other IS a problem. At least to me.

Because we investors and investment writers have to explain to the masses why owning the same thing in many different forms is not a good idea. It is just not easy for the untrained eye to see. Unless they spend more time than most investors should and will take.

Let's take TRFK, which I'm the first to cover at Seeking Alpha. Is this a "bad" ETF? Not in the fact that it appropriately targets the theme it sets out to. Here's how the fund, issued by one of the biggies in ETF-land, Pacer, describes itself.

That all sounds exciting, right? And maybe it will continue to be. But the issue to me, with this or any ETF, now that we have more than 4,000 of them in the US alone, is this: "Can I get a similar outcome in products that already exist?" The answer in this case, and in so many others I come across, is YES. Capital letters are intentional there.

Tech ETFs are everywhere. And some are more effective than others. But as in the sport of boxing, if you are going to beat the champ and take the title belt, you need to knock him out. You can't win on "points" from the judges.

Pacer, through TRFK, has every right to trot out as many ETFs as they like, no matter how much they may be just like others out there. Including their own.

Think of it this way: the biggest fund firms have done this for decades for 2 reasons: people naturally flock to something new, especially from a name they trust (think Vanguard, iShares, Invesco, etc.). Even if it is not much different than what they have. Back in the day, funds like Janus and Calamos did this well. At least for them. Shareholders simply owned 2 mutual funds that moved roughly in sync but thought they were "diversified."

And fund companies have a vested interest in allowing their managers (indexed or active) to avoid being so big that their security selection has to be limited to the very largest stocks. It's a liquidity issue in that when an ETF wants to move on from a stock, they don't want it to take multiple days since they own so much of the float.

So along comes TRFK, and it is doing what many others that came before it do. It buys companies that meet a broad definition and weights them by market cap. Then rebalances them every 3 months. Yes, they cap the individual positions at 10%, but that is not a big difference, since the biggest S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 names are below that or close to it.

That produces a portfolio that looks like this. My reaction? Haven't I seen this somewhere before? Like EVERYWHERE? Very familiar, popular, big tech names. And top-heavy like the indexes themselves. Just with a tech focus.

Which brings me to this: is TRFK just another way to own the much more tenured, easy-to-follow State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK)? Not all the time, but too often to make me want to run out and buy TRFK.

This chart shows that TRFK has had a nice run over time. But as I like to say about past performance, we can't have it. It is in the past!

This is the chart that convinces me I can just own XLK and call it a day. For the past year, the 2 ETFs have been in lockstep. That tells me that there was a brief period in which TRFK's top holdings blew the roof off the tech sector. But now that's over. Can you get a little "alpha" from TRFK? Sometimes.

But is it part of what I think many investors should focus more time on in a market climate where correlation is so high? No.

This is an expensive time to be trying to profit over a multi-year period from the tech sector. It takes more than a collection of the "usual suspects" which is what TRFK owns.

A trailing P/E of 44x, 13x trailing sales? No thanks for me. If I truly trusted the potential future earnings growth rates, maybe I'd be watching this to buy in a crash situation. But the way markets now act, I'd be better off just clicking on XLK and buying that instead. To be clear, that ETF looks dreadful to me currently as well.

The best thing I can say about this chart of TRFK is that it is flat. That's

That's better than XLK, shown here. It is starting to look "droopy" (not a technical term). But it's a step away from a leak becoming a flood.

A new ETF should be held to a high bar, a higher standard. TRFK does not do enough to merit consideration from me.

data.title: Netflix Is A Dip Worth Buying With Its Warner Bros. Acquisition
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) has over 325 million subscribers around the globe, with 98%+ retention. This is an entrenched entertainment company with immense operating leverage and long-term growth. Acquiring Warner Bros. (WBD) allows for higher engagement driven by great IP and industry-best production, large opportunities with HBO, and financial synergies across the company.

Management estimates Netflix's serviceable market is 750 million subscribers. The runway is long not only for customer acquisition but also for engagement and other offerings. Netflix launched an advertising business a few years ago; for 2026, they guided for its revenue to double to $3 billion. While SaaS companies are in a major bear market in fear of AI, Netflix content or distribution can't be vibe-coded. A high-retention subscription and advertising business on sale is hard to come by, due to their predictable and fast growth.

Netflix's business model leaves plenty of operating leverage because of slower content spend and overhead costs growth compared to topline. For years the bear case for Netflix was that it is in a constant hamster flywheel of content spending, that it could never scale profitability. Netflix has successfully blown that narrative out of the water. Over the past decade, revenue has grown at a 21% CAGR vs. content spend CAGR of 11.5% (biggest expense):

Moat

Netflix has won the "streaming wars." Disney or HBO didn't kill them. In fact, no competitor comes close to Netflix's scale. Why? They don't mind disrupting themselves. Netflix started off as a DVD-by-mail rental company before evolving to streaming. Over the past few years, they have created huge cultural shows, a low-cost ad plan for consumers (which generates similar revenue as premium tiers), very successful live events, video games, and recently exclusive video podcasts.

Take live sports, for example: Netflix didn't invest in cable like Disney. Instead, they only host high-volume events like football on holidays or celebrity WWE fights. This all combines into a must-have service for over 325 million subscribers. Smaller competitors lack the capital, scale, or execution to meaningfully gain share. Even Disney has fumbled some of its legendary IP, including Star Wars and Marvel, over the past few years.

Warner Bros. Opportunity

This is a transformative acquisition for Netflix and, surprisingly, wasn't even planned a year ago. After bids started, management decided it would be detrimental if a competitor like Paramount owned WBD and that it could be even more accretive in their own hands. For years Netflix has lacked "legacy IP." They have built amazing original shows like Squid Game or Stranger Things, but premium shows like Game of Thrones or blockbuster movies like Dune were noticeably absent from their content library. Netflix will now be able to rival the likes of Disney with a huge amount of IP at scale.

Notably, Netflix is not acquiring the Discovery portion of Warner Bros. (the decaying cable business). However, they will continue to support the theater business. Theater revenue should be supplemental, as they can monetize big projects before exclusively hosting or licensing them onto streaming platforms.

To quantify the potential media power, during the Golden Globes Nomination,

Netflix and Warner Bros dominated the event, accounting for a mighty 35 and 33 nods respectively.

What happens when a streaming service provides better content? Better engagement, which drives higher retention and ad revenue.

For the financials, this deal costs Netflix $80 billion in cash. Paramount has been rejected at least eight times, with 93% of shareholders voting against their bid, essentially removing them from the picture. The acquisition should close over the next year if approved; regulatory processes have already begun. It won't be easy, but management is confident in its ability to get this deal passed, considering Netflix faces heavy competition from Disney and especially YouTube.

In terms of the fundamentals, management expects it to be accretive to EPS after year two (2029), including $3 billion of cost synergies. For 2026, Netflix will stop buybacks to save up cash for the acquisition and will continue to use its growing FCF to maintain a healthy balance sheet, eventually resuming buybacks in a few years. The net debt/FCF will spike to ~5x but will deleverage quickly to below 3x (a healthy ratio) by 2028. Netflix will then own HBO, not one but two streaming giants. The idea is to double down on HBO premium content and, in some form, bundle it in Netflix. This also lowers licensing fees and creates a stronger ecosystem.

Financials

Management isn't opaque when it comes to communication. They clearly state on their investor website:

Our financial goals are to sustain healthy revenue growth, expand our operating margin and grow free cash flow.

Revenue has grown ~21% on average over the past decade, driven by subscriber growth and price increases. This has naturally decelerated over time. However, it recently picked up to 17% in Q4. In the long term, management wants to grow revenue 10%+. As explained earlier, there's still room to add hundreds of millions of subscribers to Netflix, with HBO duplicating the opportunity. Additionally, 55% of revenue is generated outside of North America; those markets are usually lower priced to attract new subscribers. While Netflix continues offering better value and international markets mature, there is ample pricing power left.

Management wants to reinvest its excess cash flow for the benefit of shareholders. Over the past few years, they have spent almost all profits back into reducing the share count, increasing EPS by 1-2% per year. This will resume after the debt returns to normal levels or the acquisition fails to pass.

Netflix is one of the least dilutive companies in the market, with roughly 3% of its FCF consisting of SBC (and decreasing further over time). For example, Paramount (PSKY) SBC dilutes 78% of FCF, while a mature company like Apple (AAPL) is around 10%. Low dilution helps buybacks become more effective.

Because of Netflix's organic growth, operating leverage, and buyback program, they have aggressively grown EPS by 56% annually over the past decade. While this has decelerated over time, I believe they can continue to grow it 20%+ over the next 5 years.

Valuation

Netflix is trading at a noticeable discount towards its growth and long-term prospects. Management's internal "goals" were leaked last year; during the Q4 call, they said those were through organic measures (not including WBD). These include:

  • $80b revenue, low double-digit growth
  • $30b operating income, triple in five years
  • 415m subscribers, driven by India/Brazil expansion and ad tier

All of which accumulates to their goal of reaching a $1T market cap in 2030, currently ~$325b at $76.

If you believe in management's future execution, then NFLX could return a 25% CAGR if it reaches a $1T market cap in 2030. The nuance here is that these were "goals," not guidance. Additionally, WBD fundamentally changes the company. The acquisition should accelerate earnings power and growth over the long term through cost synergies, HBO expansion, and higher engagement.

My "Fair Value" (FV) assumes 15% CAGR, or double every five years. If a stock is below FV, then it's undervalued, or vice versa. These two base case models, depending on the acquisition outcome, assume a current share price of $76:

WBD Acquisition Fallout

While the path to the acquisition is clear and Paramount is basically out of the picture, regulators still have the power to block this deal from passing. Netflix would continue its organic growth path, only paying a one-time fee of ~$6b. If they achieve their 2030 goals listed above, they can achieve 18% annual net income growth and 2% buyback yield. For a subscription and increasingly advertising company, which is growing substantially above the market with ample operating leverage, a 30x multiple is reasonable. This implies Netflix FV is around $98, ~20% higher than today's price of $76. The current price also provides a margin of safety if management falls short of expectations.

($2.63 EPS instead of $2.53 to adjust for one-time Brazilian tax hit)

Metric Base Case Assumption
Current EPS (TTM) $2.63
Proj. EPS Growth Rate (CAGR) 20%
Terminal EPS Yield 30x
2030 Projected EPS ~$6.55
2030 Stock Price Target ~$195
Implied Return (CAGR) ~20.5%
Intrinsic Value (Today) $97.5

Netflix/WBD Powerhouse

The exact financials are almost impossible to conclude this early on. However, WBD will be accretive to Netflix earnings by 2029 and onwards, according to management. Through the opportunities discussed above, I'm raising EPS growth by 5% and multiple to 33x to reflect the long-term accretive upside, bringing Netflix FV to $131 (or ~60% upside):

Metric Base Case Assumption
Current EPS (TTM) $2.63
Proj. EPS Growth Rate (CAGR) 25%
Terminal EPS Yield 33x
2030 Projected EPS ~$8
2030 Stock Price Target ~$262
Implied Return (CAGR) ~28%
Intrinsic Value (Today) $131

Risks

Netflix may not successfully maintain Warner Bros. IP and production, causing the acquisition to be value-destructive towards the company's fundamentals. This isn't a simple acquisition. Talent and relationships retention is a major risk. If top-tier directors and industry partners have trouble with these new dynamics, then production quality could decrease.

There is the "king of TV": YouTube. I don't believe YouTube is the killer of Netflix. There is a place for both in modern media. However, if engagement continues to transfer over to YouTube, engagement can suffer as a result, particularly affecting the rising advertising business.

Lastly, if the WBD acquisition simply gets blocked, that can cause a messy stock performance as the market re-rates NFLX, for better or worse. The long-term potential of Netflix decreases without WBD by having less optionality and assets, despite the company having a great thesis before the announcement.

Conclusion

I believe Netflix is a strong buy at its current price of $76. I have a 1-year price target of $100, ~30% higher. Catalysts for a re-rating could be the WBD acquisition being approved, as well as strong execution into 2026. Netflix has won the 'streaming wars,' but the path ahead is far more exciting. The opportunity with WBD is immense, and there is a clear runway of growth ahead, which is why I believe Netflix has a large terminal value and is one of my top picks.

data.title: Bitfarms: A Bitcoin Mining Company In Transition
data.description:
Bitfarms Ltd. (BITF) stands as one of the prominent players in the Bitcoin mining industry, a sector defined by high-stakes energy consumption, technological innovation, and direct correlation to cryptocurrency prices. As of the close of trading on February 13, 2026, BITF shares traded at $2.14, for a market cap of approximately $1.29 billion. The valuation reflected a Bitcoin price of a little under $69,000. Mining stocks like BITF remain under pressure from post-halving economics, rising energy costs, and competitive battles over hash rate.

The Toronto-based company was founded in 2017 and has since transitioned from a Quebec-based hydro-powered miner to a global operator with facilities stretching across North and South America. The company’s focus has been on low-cost, renewable energy sources — primarily hydroelectric power, which has positioned it as a sustainability leader in the industry. Even so, BITF’s journey has not been without ups and downs: from pandemic-era highs above $80 in 2021 (pre-split) to sub-$1 lows in 2022’s crypto winter. Investors considering taking a position in BITF today must weigh its expansion ambitions against macroeconomic headwinds like interest rates and regulatory scrutiny.

The Clarity Act will help with the latter, while interest rates are expected to come down in 2026, which has recently given a boost to the price of Bitcoin.

In this article, we'll take a look at Bitfarms’ history, operations, financial health, stock performance, and future prospects.

Company history and evolution

Bitfarms went public in 2017 on the TSX Venture Exchange under the ticker symbol of BITF. It was originally backed by a number of investors, including Digital Asset Capital Management. Initially, it used inexpensive hydropower in Quebec, Canada, to run its ASIC miners.

By 2019, it was listed on both the Toronto Stock Exchange and NASDAQ, providing the firm with the needed capital to rapidly expand operations.

The 2021 bull market allowed the company to further accelerate growth, resulting in at-the-market capital raised from convertible notes and offerings. That brought about an increase in hash rate from under 1 EH/s to over 6 EH/s by the end of the year.

All of that worked well under optimal market conditions, but in 2022, its business model was challenged by the Bitcoin crash, where the flagship crypto plummeted from roughly $69,000 to about $16,000. That forced the company to cut costs and sell some of its miners, bringing about a decline in revenues.

Among some of its major moves was when it acquired the 30 MW facility in the state of Washington, while recently diversifying itself via high-performance computing and AI workloads at the site.

Growth initiatives

From mid-2022 to early 2023, Bitcoin traded in a range of a little over $16,000 to approximately $23,000, when it finally broke out in the latter part of March 2023 and started soaring in price in October 2023. It didn't stop its upward move until it reached its all-time high two years later.

BITF wasn't able to match Bitcoin's growth trajectory during that time, although it did make a nice upward move in September 2025 before plunging in unison with the Bitcoin correction. If its share price remains subdued, it will continue to be under pressure until the price of Bitcoin begins a sustainable recovery.

While Bitcoin started to show strength again in 2023, BITF expanded into Paraguay, where it was able to operate with cheaper energy, costing about $0.03/kWh.

In 2024, the company competed at 12 EH/s of installed hash rate, with its $150 million capital raise helping to finance that growth. With the 2025 acquisition of Stronghold Digital Mining’s Pennsylvania assets for $55 million, it added some coal-refurbished sites to its holdings. Afterward, CEO Ben Gagnon stated the company had a target of 21 EH/s by the middle of 2026.

More recently, BITF sold off its 70 MW site in Paso Pe, Paraguay, to Sympatheia Power Fund for $30 million. It was to receive $9 million in cash at close, the remaining $21 million over the next ten months. Maybe the challenges of droughts in the region triggered, at least in part, its decision.

Last but not least, the company announced on February 6, 2026, that it had plans to redomicile in the U.S. from Canada, rebranding as Keel Infrastructure because of its ongoing transition from Bitcoin mining to AI infrastructure.

Even though the company reported a weak Q3 in November 2025, with revenue coming in at $69.25 million, missing by $15.41 billion, and EPS of -$0.08, missing by $0.06, this period of the company's evolution makes it difficult to value the company.

Also, gross mining margin dropped from 44% in Q3 2024 to 35% in the reporting period. The point is, more of its business will come from outside of Bitcoin mining, and how the company will be able to compete against some of its largest competitors remains to be seen.

I like the needed refocus of its business, but it has opened up another competitive front it has yet to prove its long-term execution and performance in.

While true, BITF COO Liam Wilson is bullish on the prospects of the company, stating at the 28th Annual Needham Growth Conference, January 13, 2026, that "our megawatts are harder to get in higher demand areas, produce more value for customers, and are worth more per megawatt."

If the company can scale that, it could be a growth engine for the prolonged future.

Conclusion

BITF launched as a vertically integrated, inexpensive miner to scale globally. While it will remain a Bitcoin mining operation for some time, that will undoubtedly decline in importance as revenue and earnings from that unit of the company decline going forward.

As mentioned earlier, growth will increasingly come from demand for data infrastructure driven by AI, as well as demand for high‑performance computing (HPC). The market, in general, likes what the company is doing, but its success is far from being guaranteed.

Not only is competition in the Bitcoin mining sector brutal, with larger competitors like CleanSpark, Core Scientific, Marathon, and RIOT dominating the sector, scaling far beyond BITF. That means it must compete on lower costs when mining BTC-USD, extremely efficient facility management, and expansion in a way that doesn't totally rely upon hash rate in and of itself. In the AI compute segment of the market, the competition is even more fierce, where BITF goes beyond directly competing with larger Bitcoin mining firms to competing with large data-center operators, cloud providers, and GPU startups with significant backing from venture capital.

For the company to succeed, it must compete within the confines of its cost structure. If it can do so with GPU hosting and/or HPC workloads, it could potentially thrive in that specific niche of the market.

To do so, management has to execute close to flawlessly. It could possibly get by with a few smaller hiccups, but any large mistake against the powerhouses it's competing against on the mining, hosting, and GPU sides of the business, and it would likely struggle to recover.

If that were to play out, the best hope for shareholders would be for the company to be acquired.

And speaking of being acquired, I tend to think the steps it is taking are anticipating a potential offer from a larger suitor.

It could definitely thrive in the niche it's gravitating toward, again, assuming it can execute at a high level, but I think there's a ceiling on its upside growth in the future as the company stands, and there isn't much clarity on what it'll do if and when it successfully executes its current business plan.
